summary: McKenzie describes A/B testing a redesigned signup page for Bingo Card Creator, replacing a spartan Wufoo-derived form with a more visually engaging design featuring a sidebar for conversion arguments. He also addresses the jarring transition users experience when moving from themed mini-sites (like Halloween bingo) to the main site by customizing the signup page to match the referring mini-site's branding and theme.
key_points: A visually engaging signup page with a sidebar for persuasive content can improve conversions over a spartan form
Maintaining 'scent' — visual continuity between a landing page and signup page — prevents users from bouncing when they feel they've landed in the wrong place
Small investments in design consistency across domains (matching colors, headers, imagery) reduce friction in the conversion funnel
Even functional designs that empirically convert well may benefit from testing against more polished alternatives
money_quotes: I like this because it is more visually engaging and contains the sidebar, which I can stuff full of reasons they should sign up without impacting the readability of the main form.

The transition from that page to my site was sort of jarring — you move over domains, which normal users could care less about, but you also move from a black and orange color scheme to my soothing pastel blues.
I was worried about losing the 'scent' — having a user figure 'Whoops, this isn't getting me to what I wanted' and closing out prior to signing up.
It empirically works (fairly well, actually) but it is spartan.
All I have to do is upload a header image and write a wee bit of CSS and, presto-changeo.
key_insight: Maintaining visual continuity ('scent') between landing pages and signup pages prevents users from bouncing when the design shift makes them doubt they're in the right place.
